# Context

This project analyzes the impact of various supplements on muscle mass gain in individuals undergoing resistance training. A systematic review was conducted on PubMed, as shown in `output/diagrama_revision.png`. Results were manually extracted and standardized to %LBM (% Lean Body Mass), generating `data/datos_limpios.csv`. A meta-analysis was then performed using mean difference (MD).

# Project Information

## Structure
* `/data`: Contains all .csv files used during the analysis.
* `/output`: Contains all .png files generated by R.
* `metaanalisis.Rmd`: Notebook where the analysis was performed.

## How to view the report?
Download `metaanalisis.html` and open it in any browser, or view it directly here:
[View report](https://rubengil-dev.github.io/metaanalisis-suplementacion-hipertrofia/)

It covers all the statistical concepts needed to understand the meta-analysis. If you have no background on the topic, read the "Understanding the Meta-Analysis" section below.

# Understanding the Meta-Analysis

## Study Selection and Data Extraction

### Search
The exact search performed on PubMed was:
```
((muscle hypertrophy[Title/Abstract]) OR (muscle growth[Title/Abstract]) OR (skeletal muscle mass[Title/Abstract]) OR (lean body mass[Title/Abstract]) OR (free fat mass[Title/Abstract])) AND ((supplementation[Title/Abstract]) OR (ergogenic aid[Title/Abstract]) OR (ergogenic supplement[Title/Abstract]) OR (supplement intake[Title/Abstract]) OR (sports supplements[Title/Abstract]) OR (dietary supplements[Title/Abstract]) OR (creatine monohydrate[Title/Abstract]) OR (beta-HMB[Title/Abstract]) OR (beta-hydroxy-beta-methylbutyrate[Title/Abstract]) OR (protein[Title/Abstract]) OR (essential amino acids[Title/Abstract]) OR (beta-alanine[Title/Abstract]) OR (caffeine[Title/Abstract]) OR (sodium supplements[Title/Abstract])) AND ((resistance training[Title/Abstract]) OR (strength training[Title/Abstract]) OR (RET[Title/Abstract])) NOT (disease[Title/Abstract]) NOT (disorder[Title/Abstract]) NOT (syndrome[Title/Abstract]) NOT (surgery[Title/Abstract]) NOT (chronic[Title/Abstract]) NOT (injury[Title/Abstract]) NOT (pain[Title/Abstract]) NOT (sarcopenia[Title/Abstract]) NOT (cachexia[Title/Abstract]) NOT (obesity[Title/Abstract]) NOT (overweight[Title/Abstract]) NOT (diabetes[Title/Abstract]) NOT (metabolic syndrome[Title/Abstract]) NOT (metabolic disorder[Title/Abstract]) NOT (neurodegenerative disease[Title/Abstract]) NOT (dementia[Title/Abstract]) NOT (cancer[Title/Abstract]) NOT (stroke[Title/Abstract]) NOT (rats[Title/Abstract]) NOT (mouse[Title/Abstract])
```
### Inclusion Criteria
* Studies were randomized controlled trials (RCT) comparing a supplement against a placebo.
* Studies were published between January 2015 and June 2025 (inclusive).
* Studies involved the use of one or more ergogenic supplements combined with a resistance training program (RET).
* Studies were conducted in healthy adults (+18) with no restriction on sex or training level.

### Exclusion Criteria
* Participants had any health condition.
* Participants had obesity or physical limitations preventing resistance training.
* Full text was not available.
* The study outcome did not measure lean body mass (LBM).
* The study design did not include double-blinding.
* The study compared one supplement against another.

### Outcome
The search returned 865 studies (+5 found through bibliography review), of which 26 were finally included.

## Data Extraction
All 26 articles were manually reviewed and data extracted, with ∆%LBM as the primary outcome. For studies that did not directly report this result, sections 6.5.2.2 and 6.2.5.8 of the Cochrane Handbook were followed.

## Statistical Analysis
The `meta`, `metafor` and `dmetar` libraries were used to perform a mean difference (MD) meta-analysis and outlier analysis. Subgroup analyses and meta-regressions were also conducted.

* MD is always reported with its 95% confidence interval (95% CI).
* A random effects model (REM) was applied, assuming each study introduces an additional source of variability (heterogeneity).
* Subgroups analyzed: Age, Sex, Supplementation timing, Study duration, Supplement used, Participants' training status.
* Meta-regressions were performed for age and duration independently, and a global meta-regression.

## Results

* Outlier analysis identified "Lowery RP 2016", "Mobley CB_01 2017", "Holwerda AM 2018", and "Eghbali E 2024" as outside the MD confidence interval and were excluded.
* Both Egger's test (p = 0.803) and funnel plots indicate no publication bias among selected studies.

### Meta-Analysis Results (post outlier removal)

* **Global effect:** The supplemented group gained 0.72% more LBM (95% CI: 0.26 – 1.19). Statistically significant, but the magnitude is negligible.
* **Heterogeneity:** Outlier removal reduced initial heterogeneity from 93.4% to 47.1%, meaning 4 out of 26 studies accounted for nearly 50% of heterogeneity. Their exclusion is justified.

### Subgroup Analysis Results
To assess whether any variable directly influences the observed effect, Chi² was examined. A p-value below 0.05 would indicate meaningful differences between subgroups. All subgroups except supplement type had p-values above 0.05, confirming none of them influence the result.

Within the supplement subgroup, only protein showed a statistically significant p-value.

**Conclusion:** Of all variables analyzed, only supplement type is truly relevant for muscle mass gain — and within supplements, only protein is statistically effective, with an effect of 0.77% LBM gain versus placebo.

### Meta-Regression
The goal was to assess whether any variable linearly relates to the observed effect. The coefficient explains this relationship, but is only relevant when the moderator test (QM) is significant. Since all QM p-values across the 3 regressions exceeded 0.05, neither age, study duration, nor all variables combined directly explain the observed effect. This is consistent with R² values of 0 (no heterogeneity explained) and high QE values (remaining unexplained heterogeneity).

## Limitations

* Only English-language publications indexed in PubMed were included; other databases were not reviewed.
* Many variables relevant to hypertrophy were not analyzed: training frequency, type of resistance training, supplement dosage...
* Analyzing a single outcome variable does not provide a comprehensive view of muscle gain.
* Low participant counts in most RCTs, lack of sex differentiation in many, and unspecified ethnicity may impact the analysis.
* As a personal conclusion: studying supplementation broadly may be a methodological error, as the scope is too large and heterogeneous.

## Conclusions

1. Supplementation has a positive effect on LBM gain.
2. Factors such as age, sex, study duration, prior training status, and supplementation timing do not influence LBM gain.
3. The type of supplement used can alter results, with protein being the most effective supplement.

*However, these findings should be interpreted with caution given the limitations outlined above.*
